turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court in case 16-1140, NIFLA versus Becerra.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a Writ of Certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. The California Reproductive Freedom, Accountability, Comprehensive Care and Transparency Act was enacted to regulate crisis pregnancy centers which are, for the most part, pro-life organizations that offer pregnancy related services. The Act requires clinics primarily serving pregnant women to provide certain notices. Licensed clinics must notify women that the State of California provides certain services, including abortions, and must give them a phone number to call. Unlicensed clinics must notify women that California has not licensed them to provide medical services. Petitioners filed suit alleging that both notices abridged the freedom of speech protected by the First Amendment. The district court denied their motion for preliminary injunction and the Ninth Circuit affirmed. In an opinion on file with the clerk today we reverse. Starting with the licensed notice, we hold that it likely violates the First Amendment. Content-based laws are presumptively unconstitutional and may be justified only if they satisfy strict scrutiny. The licensed notice requirement is a content-based regulation because it compels petitioners to speak a particular message. Although this requirement is content-based, the Ninth Circuit did not apply strict scrutiny because it concluded that the notice regulates professional speech. But this Court has never recognized such a category of speech. Speech is not unprotected merely because it is uttered by professionals. This Court has afforded less protection for professional speech in two circumstances; commercial disclosures that are purely factual and uncontroversial and regulations of professional conduct. Neither of these categories turns on the fact that professionals are speaking and neither is implicated here. Beyond these two contexts our precedents have long protected the First Amendment rights of professionals. No party or court has advanced a persuasive reason for now applying different rules to an amorphous category known as professional speech. Such a reason may well exist. Here though we do not need to consider the question further because the licensed notice requirement cannot satisfy even intermediate scrutiny. The notice is underinclusive, perhaps widely so. It does not apply to similar types of clinics that are just as likely to serve the low income women that California says it wants to inform, and California has less intrusive ways to inform women about its services, such as its own public information campaign. We hold that the unlicensed notice also likely violates the First Amendment. It is unnecessary to decide whether the standard for commercial disclosures established by our decision in Zauderer applies. Even under Zauderer, the unlicensed disclosure fails because it is unjustified and unduly burdensome. The only stated justification for the unlicensed notice is ensuring that pregnant women know when they are receiving medical care from licensed professionals. But California disavowed that justification at oral argument. Even if it stood by this justification, the unlicensed notice requirement is an unduly burdensome method of achieving it. The unlicensed notice imposes a government scripted, speaker-based disclosure requirement that is wholly disconnected from California's supposed interests. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Ninth Circuit. Justice Kennedy has filed a concurring opinion, in which the Chief Justice, Justice Alito and Justice Gorsuch have joined. Justice Breyer has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justices Ginsburg, Sotomayor and Kagan have joined.
